title: RA.991 BADSISTA

translation.content: BADSISTA doesn't do stasis. In fact, he prefers to be in a constant state of motion. It was immediately obvious when the São Paulo artist born Rafaela Andrade broke out with his 2016 self-titled EP, in which a bass-heavy melange of baile funk, dembow and trap demonstrated an ability to satiate almost any dance floor. But BADSISTA continued to evolve through the different moods and textures of the club: from experimental compositions with Brazilian trans icon Linn Da Quebrada to ballroom bass and heads-down funk shellers for TraTraTrax. This RA Podcast finds Andrade in a fluid place once more, a showcase of experimentation and new sounds. The mood starts out slow and moody—including a slew of unreleased BADSISTA tracks—before seamlessly morphing into the soul-stirring synthwork of Al Lover Meets Cairo Liberation Front. Then he gets playful: tasteful, techy house morphs into smutty baile funk. (And as for the guests, look out for Sully's "XT" and a spicy Batu rework.) BADSISTA's style is connected by a uniquely Latin sense of rhythm and groove. Here, dramatic synths build up to even more dramatic funk crescendos. Perreo rattles appear in and out of the mix, as if acting as a reminder for people to move. All it takes is ten seconds of "DOS HERMANOS," with its shakers and whistle samples, to jolt even the stiffest hips into activity. But really, above all, this mix radiates with aliveness. The kind of flesh-and-bone euphoria that prickles the skin when leaving it all on a dance floor. "It's by moving our body and mind that make being here worth it," he simply put it. And it's true: when you whittle it down to the bare essentials, all that matters is the joy and connectivity you feel within yourself and the world around you. BADSISTA is an excellent facilitator, and you'll hear as much on RA.991. – Rachel Grace Almeida Where was the mix recorded? Can you tell us the idea behind it? I recorded this with a Pioneer DDJ-400 controller in my São Paulo home studio. I'm a very shy person, but I love dancing and getting lost in loud music. I've been looking for a new kind of sound for a while now—this mix showcases a lot of the experimentation that I've been doing the last few months. Layers, rhythm, silence... the hypnosis of sound. How do you want to see your sound evolve? I've always learned by watching other people—and I've seen a lot in recent years. Being in so many places has allowed me to open my eyes to music more and understand how each person connects with music in such an intimate and unique way. I've come to believe that the best paths are the simple ones that are lovingly cared for. That's how I see my sound [evolving]—with more affection, confidence and maturity. It's great to look in the mirror and recognise yourself, to understand that place of discovery. It feels like all eyes are on baile funk right now; so many people are waking up to the sound and all its variations. Who are some funk artists and crews from back home you want to shout out? Bonekinha Iraquiana, DJ Dayeh, D.silvestre and DJ Halc are raising the bar for São Paulo funk. They have a very loyal audience. I'm very interested in their acid and bass sounds. Two crews that have been making a lot of noise and working hard for the scene: Submundo808 and Met&Rala. Crews like these allow new artists the space to show up. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Respect for human beings. Just as the Indigenous people of Brazil were almost wiped out, Palestinian people are suffering from unreasonable violence, losing their right to life and their very humanity. I want to also include trans people who have lost so much in recent months. I hope that one day we will no longer be afraid of losing our right to live as people who matter. What's one message you want people to take away from this mix? That my greatest loyalty is to music. Music has given me the chance to be someone different and inspire people around the world. Moving our body and mind makes it worth it being here.
translation.blurb: Club futurism and a stack of new material from one of São Paulo's boldest shapeshifters.
